speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number two. Today is January 2, 1997. I'm interviewing Shirley Warman. You were talking about a Polish friend that had hidden you. And you left the apartment because she said something to you. Can you say again what she said, and what you felt about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: She came downstairs to the basement. And she was sitting, talking to me. And then I said, what's going on over there in the street? She said, it's a lot of Germans. And you don't see anybody but the Jewish people. Then she said, well, look, not a big thing. If I would have the power, and-- I would go and tell them where all the Jewish people are hiding. I'd tell them, look, I'm a Jewish-- I'm Jewish girl, too. She said, I don't mean you. I said, listen to me. Thank you very much. I appreciate what you've done for me. And I'm going to go up to-- to my parents. Whatever it's going to be with them, it's going to be with me, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she say after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was anybody in your family ever taken by the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, not this time. Not in my time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was anybody taken for forced labor? Did they pull anybody from your family or friends that you know for forced labor?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else happened when the Germans first came in to Lublin at the beginning?

speaker: interviewee
text: The beginning, well, there was no food altogether right away. And they start-- you couldn't go out. When you go out-- you know, the city was full of fire, was burning. And if you start to run, they start to shoot. You were afraid they'd start to shoot, that's all. I wasn't too long with them, the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was happening in your personal life at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: In your family, did you have your friends or a boyfriend at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had boyfriend, yes. And-- and we started to talk, to run away. You know, to go-- as my-- my father said, if you want to go, you gotta get married. So we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Benjamin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old was he when you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was 21.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what date did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939, December 25.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you run away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We both was hiring a wagon with horses, and they took us to Parczew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who arranged it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you talk a little bit about how that worked, how he arranged it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know. No. We didn't talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you leave Lublin?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939. December-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think the-- the 26th, the 27th.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go into that, I'd just like to go back a moment. What type of marriage did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we got married by-- in my parents' house. Just the family was there, and his-- and his family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you decide to run away?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't stand it, to be like that. Not to go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so what did you plan?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't plan anything. We start to run, and that's it. Whatever comes out, comes out. You couldn't plan anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were on your way to Parczew, did anything happen on the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were hiding. They covered us with blankets. And it was quiet. It was cold already. And we came to Parczew and we were staying with my mother's brother-- and not too long. Then we start again.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was Parczew from the city of Lublin?

speaker: interviewee
text: A night with a-- with a wagon, with horses to go. One night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father have to pay for this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I didn't ask questions.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened in Parczew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we couldn't stay there. Everybody was, you know, talking and hiding. So we start to run again. And we came to Lutsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you start to run? What did you use to run?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were some people, what they start-- at night-- that started to provide people to go. Just at night we went, over the-- over the forest and this, and we came to Lutsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were walking all the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. All the way walking. Just hiding and a little bit rest and a little bit walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was very little. We were afraid to go in to ask for food. You know-- you were afraid, if you open a door, and who knows what could be? But we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the way to Lutsk, were there any incidents that happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were hiding in the woods, in the forest, into the trees. And was no-- a lot of people was running. It was not just us. There was a lot of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was a lot, maybe about 100 or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it organized? Or they--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Everybody was-- we all went together. There was no organized things.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get to Lutsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to Lutsk in the-- first of-- first-- 1940, just the 1st.

speaker: interviewer
text: January 1?

speaker: interviewee
text: January 1, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you cross the border?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't easy. The-- how do I-- They took us-- the Russians took-- you know, at night. We crossed the border. And one side was the Germans and one side was the Russians. And we made it to the Russian side. It was the middle of the night. They took us in. There was a lot, lots of people there. And they start [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know-- they start to make-- there was little rooms. Like little barracks-- I don't know what they called them, like closets. And they start to take, you know, who's going to go back and who's going to-- this closet, people is going to go back to the Germans. This one goes to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was doing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians. The Russians. And then we saw what's going on. My husband said to me like this, listen to me. Let's stay together. Hold on. And whatever they're going to do with us, we're going to be together. And our path became that we stayed in Russia. You know, they didn't send us back.

speaker: interviewer
text: To go back just a little bit, how were you able to cross the border?

speaker: interviewee
text: People were helping us to cross the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know who they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Jewish people, I don't know. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you pay them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you pay them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some zlotys, you know-- we had the money, zlotys. We had some money. My father gave it to me. And we paid a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you when you started to run?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the things which-- which you was wearing and, you know, just a little bit-- the same shoes, just a sweater, and an overcoat. That's all. Nothing else. We  are afraid, somebody is going to, you know, see us going out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you become friendly with anybody else while you were running away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Talking, just talking. You know, everybody was their own problems.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: How we're going to survive. How we're going to do it. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did people say? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same thing. Everybody had their own problems. A lot of them was with children. It was very hard for them, very hard. And a child started to cry. But we were the lucky ones-- we had no children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when the children started to cry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Started to tell them stop. What else can they tell them? They put the hand on their mouth and said, please don't cry. One said, I'm hungry. One said, I have to go to the toilet. But life was very cruel. It wasn't pleasant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before your left Lublin to get to Lutsk, was anything said to you by your family members?

speaker: interviewee
text: Do what your hearts say. You want to go, go. We're not going to stop you. I said, my father, why can't all go together? He said, no. I can't go. I remember from the First World War what the Russian-- there was no good. The Germans was better this time. So maybe we gonna-- it's going to be OK. You come back to us. That's what they said. This was the last word that I spoke to them. I never saw them back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in Russia is Lutsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far from the border?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you got to the Russian side, you were all taken in different compartments?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Then they took us all together. You know-- in Lutsk-- no, they let us go. And everybody went to look where to stay, where to sleep, and where to find work.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were let go, you and your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a lot of people. Oh, hundreds and hundreds of people went out. And a lot of them, they sent back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who offered you any help when you were in Lutsk? How did you find housing?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were just walking around looking. And a lot of people, you know, we start to get friendly. We start to find people what went to Lublin. And they said, let's go in here. And we went into-- into one house. And she was all alone, the woman. And she took us in. She took in about eight, about 10 people. We were sleeping on the floors. And this was the life, there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in Lutsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrive-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in January, but I don't know. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the house that you lived in?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a little house. You know, there was nothing to it. There was-- was no beds. We all slept on the floors. And that's it. And then my husband went out, he find work. He was working.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He made the tops from the shoes with a machine.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a shoemaker?

speaker: interviewee
text: A shoemaker. And you know, you have to stay in the line to buy some bread, to buy anything. And life was nothing to it. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you say nothing, what do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't have no desire for anything. Like, we  lived like dogs, that's all. Can you describe it more in detail, about your life in Lutsk? You didn't have no privacy. You didn't have no clothes what to wear. You didn't have where to go, you know, to get washed up. There was a public place-- you gotta go until you go in, until you get washed, until you go out. This was no life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody offer you any help?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Everybody was in the same shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the woman whose house you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who knows? Who remembers? We paid her. If we did not pay, she wouldn't let us in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your husband get paid for his work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, he worked. He got paid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any religious Jewish life in Lutsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. We didn't look for it. And that-- we could-- it was no use for this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people have any observances?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were no--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have no interest in this altogether.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was happening to you that you lost interest?

speaker: interviewee
text: The life what we had. What kind of interest can you have when you have a life like that? I would always say to myself, someday I'm going to go back to my parents. This kept me-- nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you adjust to the Soviet system? What was happening with the Soviets in terms of their attitudes to the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have nothing to do with them. We didn't talk. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your husband say about his work?

speaker: interviewee
text: He liked it. It was survival. It was survival. You try day by day to survive. That was the whole thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet any other people that you became friendly with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, you know, where you could stay in the line to buy a piece of bread or buy some sugar, some anything. So we got friendly, but everybody went their ways.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never found work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you not want to? Or you couldn't?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have no interest.

speaker: interviewer
text: You sounded like you were depressed.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Yeah, I didn't have no interest for anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were free--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: From-- from the Germans? Yes, yes. And what was the loss of interest?

speaker: interviewee
text: You saw what kind of life that we had there. It was no life. There was no life altogether. You-- you were afraid to say anything. Maybe somebody is going to hear anything, what you're saying. You live like a dog, like-- like no human.

speaker: interviewer
text: If somebody did say something and they were overheard, what would happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who knows what they did to them? Very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No, I stayed away from everything and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was life in the house that you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: What kind of life could it be? We were 10 people, and everybody was hungry. And everybody was talking. And this was-- they got sick, and this was-- it was horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get sick there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did get sick there?

speaker: interviewee
text: People got sick. They couldn't take it. I always said to myself, someday-- someday I'm going to tell what I went through. I'm going to survive. And this kept me. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else did you experience in Lutsk that we didn't discuss?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing else. Nothing else. Nothing. The people were good to us. You know, they felt sorry for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which people are we talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lutsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: The population there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Population, yes. And they start to letting us-- you know, there was a line to get a piece of bread or buy some sugar or buy a bone from the cow to make soup. So they let us in first. They said, I bet you you never had this in Poland. You see? They did, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, the Jewish community.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they offer any other assistance?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the Russians, the Soviets?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't see. The Russian wasn't there. They let it go. This was, you know, [NON-ENGLISH] Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of political system did--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I wasn't involved.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your husband involved?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not this time. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Another time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Lutsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came, 1940. And in July, I don't know how long we stayed. A year or more. I did-- I know in June or July it was very hot, and everybody-- and the Russians-- like over here, the FBI, they call the [NON-ENGLISH], the Russians. They came to the houses, and they took everybody out.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was July of what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1940. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 1940 or '41? '41, 1941. Yes, they took us all out. And they took us into the police station.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was middle of the night. We went out. They said, get dressed. And we got dressed. And they took us. And they asked if we have-- if we have citizen papers, passports. We said no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to have anything, any papers when you lived in Lutsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no. We didn't have anything. We left home with nothing. So they took us out, and everybody was thinking they just take-- take us, not somebody else. But they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did they take out?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took me, they took my husband, and they started to take everyone from the-- from this house. Then, we started to say something in Jewish. Why-- why are they taking us? Is something wrong? Then we find out they took everyone.

speaker: interviewer
text: All the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the--the newcomers, when they came to Lutsk. Not that was living there people. They took us to the police station. And they started to ask questions.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, where is your children? I said, we have no children. They took me one place in one room, they took my husband in the other. I didn't know what he was saying, and he didn't know what I was saying. And this was it. Well, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then you know, this was first or the second-- they took us, the whole people were the newcomers. They took us in a big place. A big-- it was a-- it wasn't a park, it was just a place, outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: Outside where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lutsk. Outside, you know. And they start to talk with microphones. They said, who wants-- who wants to take citizen papers so that they could stay? And who doesn't want it, they're going to send us this-- they're going to send us someplace. They--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they say where they were going to send you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't say anything. Well, afterwards, everybody said, we have-- we want to go home. You send us back to Poland, to the Germans. The Germans was there. And they said, OK, we don't want to take-- a lot of people took citizen papers from them. But they came out the same thing what we do.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know about the Soviet citizen laws?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know anything. We couldn't speak to them. We didn't understand when they  were talking, but there was translation. And we said, OK, we want to go home. This was before. Then they took us out at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just to go back a moment. They made an announcement in the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they do that? Was it during the day or night?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what. Well, they came in the middle of the night, that's true. And they took us out. And they put us on the trains. This was already, who didn't want to take the citizen papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So first they asked if you wanted to--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. First they ask, and we didn't take the papers. So they took us to the-- on the-- on the buses, like, you know, on the trucks. And they took us to the station, to the trains. And I don't-- I didn't know where my husband went. And they put us in, hundreds of people, on these cattle trains, and they didn't let us out. This was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you say that you and your husband were separated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a chance to say goodbye to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't see him. They took us out. They took him in room  one, and they took me in the other room. And this was-- people when they had children, they went together. Without children, it was separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you told the Russians when they questioned you that you would prefer to go back to your family, how did they react to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They said, OK. We'll send you back. They did say, we're sending all of you people back to the Germans, to your parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they came back in the middle of the night and--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they took us already to the train. They said, when you're taken out, you should-- we should-- we're going to send us back to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the same night that you were asked--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the same night they sent us away, you know, to Siberia. We didn't know where we were going.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Just whatever you were wearing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was wearing, like, it was hot, warm. And I had a dress. I had shoes. No stockings. And that's it. Whatever you got on your shoulders, this is what you were wearing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that the last time you saw your husband at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: That point, I saw the last time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you feeling? Or how did you react to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no feeling. There was no feeling at all. Everybody was for themselves to survive. There was no feelings.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what date were you deported from Lutsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was warm. I don't-- July, August. I don't know. It was warm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they tell you, the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're going to send you back.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that point, you thought you were going back to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of transportation did they put you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In cattle trains, for the, you know, for the cattles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they open or closed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I mean, the doors were open. There was no windows. It was just, you know, like a little-- put eye in, just to look out a little bit. And the-- the Russians were staying, and they were looking after us. We couldn't-- we couldn't go down. We couldn't escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were with you in one car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hundreds and hundreds of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in your car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in my car. There was plenty of cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how many were in your car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who knows? I don't know. 20, 30. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say that the Russians were guarding the car.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated by them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was no treated. You know, a whole day you were inside. At night, they opened the doors. And they brought us bread and water to eat. That's it. You couldn't wash up. Everybody was running into the trains, you know. There was no toilets. There was a hole in the-- in the car, and we were ashamed. So a lot of people had blankets or something. And they made it, if everybody wants to go, you know, they just covered it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And everybody slept together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what arrangement was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no arrangement. It was made like from wood, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: On the cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, inside. And everybody would find a place to lay down to sleep. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there children there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They were crying, and what can you do? Nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you talking to anyone on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We were talking. Some of them wanted to talk. Some of them said don't bother me, I have my own problems, and let me think. And this was the whole conversation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stop at any time or get off the trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We stopped in a lot of cities, but I don't know the cities. Villages, most of the time villages. A lot of Russians came to the trains. They were thinking, maybe we have something. They were looking for something. But we didn't have anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that point, did you think you were going back to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little while. But afterwards, some people were speaking Russian. And the soldiers told them, you're not going back. We don't know where. They tell us all the time that we're going in the day or night, then they tell us where to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue on the next tape.
